coredata.dc:title: Box-Behnken design optimization of a green fluorescence sensor based on S,N-Doped graphene quantum dots for glimepiride determination in pharmaceuticals and biological samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alqahtani, Ali
Alqahtani, Taha
Fatease, Adel Al
Alshehri, Adil
Almrasy, Ahmed A.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a fluorescent sensor based on sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) was developed for the determination of glimepiride in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma. The spectral characteristics of the sensor were investigated revealing a blue fluorescence emission at 431 nm upon excitation at 362 nm. Stern-Volmer analysis indicated that the quenching of the sensor’s fluorescence by glimepiride followed a static quenching mechanism, which was supported by thermodynamic studies. A Box-Behnken experimental design was employed to optimize the factors affecting the quenching process, including pH, buffer volume, S,N-GQDs concentration and incubation time. A quadratic model was obtained, and the optimum conditions were determined. The sensor exhibited a linear response to glimepiride in the range of 0.5–4.0 μg/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.078 μg/mL. The developed method was validated according to ICH guidelines and successfully applied for the determination of glimepiride in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples. Additionally, the greenness and blueness of the method was evaluated using the AGREE and BAGI metrics revealing a high level of environmental friendliness and analytical practicability posing the developed sensor as a sustainable analytical tool.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glimepiride
Fluorescence quenching
S,N-GQDs
DoE
Green Chemistry

Application to pharmaceutical formulations and plasma samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (RGP2/399/45).
The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Research Project under grant number RGP2/399/45.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The excitation and emission spectra of the S,N-GQDs showing a strong fluorescence emission peak at 431 nm when excited at 362 nm. (B) The effect of different glimepiride concentrations on the S,N-GQDs' fluorescence demonstrating a concentration-dependent quenching effect.

(A) Stern-Volmer plots depicting the relationship between F0/F and glimepiride concentrations (μM) at three different temperatures (298 K, 303 K, and 313 K). The decreasing slope with increasing temperature suggests a static quenching mechanism. (B) Van 't Hoff plot illustrating the temperature dependence of the binding constant (Ka), shown as ln(Ka) versus 1/T (K−1).

(A) 2D interaction plot showing the relationship between F0/F and pH at different volumes of S,N-GQDs (mL). The curves suggest a non-linear interaction between pH and GQD volume on F0/F. (B) 3D surface plot illustrating the combined effects of pH and GQD volume on F0/F. The plot reveals a complex relationship with F0/F increasing at higher pH and S,N-GQDs volumes.

Selectivity of S,N-GQDs towards glimepiride in the presence of potential interfering compounds. The bar graph shows the fluorescence quenching factor [(F0-F)/F0] for glimepiride and various other substances, including common ions, excipients, and pooled plasma. Glimepiride exhibits a significantly higher quenching factor compared to all other tested compounds, demonstrating the high selectivity of the S,N-GQDs sensor for glimepiride detection.

Assessing the environmental impact of (A) the developed method, (B) the reported HPLC chromatographic method and (C) the reported LC-MS/MS method using the AGREE tool. Blueness assessment of (D) the developed method, (E) the reported HPLC chromatographic method and (F) the reported LC-MS/MS method using the BAGI tool to determine their analytical practicability.
ANOVA table for studying the factors affecting the quenching process between S,N-GQDs and glimepiride using Box-Behnken design and applying backward elimination.
Validation data for determination of glimepiride by the developed S,N-GQDs fluorescent probe.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured on the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured in the three consecutive days.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitative analysis of glimepiride in the commercial Amaryl® tablets by the proposed method and statistical comparison with the reported method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in parenthesis are tabulated values of “t “and “F” at (P = 0.05).

head.ce:title: Box-Behnken design optimization of a green fluorescence sensor based on S,N-Doped graphene quantum dots for glimepiride determination in pharmaceuticals and biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha 62529 Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha 62529 Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha 62529, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Cairo 11751 Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Cairo 11751, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a fluorescent sensor based on sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) was developed for the determination of glimepiride in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma. The spectral characteristics of the sensor were investigated revealing a blue fluorescence emission at 431 nm upon excitation at 362 nm. Stern-Volmer analysis indicated that the quenching of the sensor’s fluorescence by glimepiride followed a static quenching mechanism, which was supported by thermodynamic studies. A Box-Behnken experimental design was employed to optimize the factors affecting the quenching process, including pH, buffer volume, S,N-GQDs concentration and incubation time. A quadratic model was obtained, and the optimum conditions were determined. The sensor exhibited a linear response to glimepiride in the range of 0.5–4.0 μg/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.078 μg/mL. The developed method was validated according to ICH guidelines and successfully applied for the determination of glimepiride in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples. Additionally, the greenness and blueness of the method was evaluated using the AGREE and BAGI metrics revealing a high level of environmental friendliness and analytical practicability posing the developed sensor as a sustainable analytical tool.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of nanoparticles are of significant interest for analytical applications due to their unique sensing capabilities [1,2]. of all nanoparticles, quantum dots have garnered significant attention for their outstanding optical and electronic properties [3]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in particular, are a promising class of carbon-based nanomaterials that exhibit excellent photostability, biocompatibility, and tunable photoluminescence [4]. In addition, their optical transitions can be extended up to the ultraviolet range, making them suitable for a variety of applications including photodetection and fluorescence sensing [5]. In terms of environmental friendliness and cost-effective production, GQDs can be easily synthesized from various carbon sources such as citric acid and glucose posing them as attractive analytical tools [6]. Another attractive feature of GQDs is the ability to engineer their surface properties through heteroatom doping, which can further enhance their sensitivity and selectivity for analytical applications [7]. Several reports have demonstrated the use of GQDs doped with boron, nitrogen, and sulfur for the sensitive detection of organic pollutants, heavy metal ions, and biological analytes [8,9]. Also, the use of GQDs for the detection of pharmaceutical compounds has been gaining increasing interest [10].

Spectrofluorimetry has been a widely used analytical technique for the determination of drug analytes due to its high sensitivity, ease of use, and minimal sample preparation requirements [11]. In addition, low energy consumption as well as minimal waste production further add to the environmental sustainability of the technique [12]. Hence, the coupling of GQDs with spectrofluorimetric detection presents a promising approach for the sensitive and selective determination of pharmaceutical compounds in biological samples and formulations [13]. For example, a recent study has reported the use of boron and nitrogen co-doped GQDs for the ultrasensitive detection of tetracycline in environmental and biological samples [14]. However, several factors such as pH, GQDs concentration and reaction time can influence the detection process and need to be optimized to achieve maximum sensitivity [15]. Design of experiments (DoE) based on statistical methods such as the Box-Behnken approach has been widely used for the systematic optimization of analytical methods, allowing for the evaluation of interactive effects between parameters and reduced experimental efforts [16]. Hence, such an approach can be particularly useful for the development of a sensitive GQDs-based spectrofluorimetric assay [17].

Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder that affects millions of people worldwide [18]. Glimepiride is a widely prescribed second-generation sulfonylurea drug used for the management of type II diabetes [19]. Due to its low therapeutic index, the accurate determination of glimepiride in biological samples and pharmaceutical formulations is crucial for therapeutic drug monitoring and quality control. Literature reports the use of various analytical techniques such as HPLC [20], LC-MS/MS [21], spectrophotometry [22,23] and electrochemical sensors [24] for the determination of glimepiride. However, chromatographic methods such as HPLC and LC-MS/MS often suffer from complex sample preparation, the need for sophisticated instrumentation, and high waste generation. On the other hand, spectrophotometric methods can lack the required sensitivity for the accurate quantification of glimepiride in complex matrices as well as the need for extra derivatization steps. Interestingly, to the best of our knowledge, no reports exist on the use of GQDs for the spectrofluorimetric determination of glimepiride.

Hence, the present study aims to develop a sensitive and selective spectrofluorimetric method for the determination of glimepiride in human plasma and pharmaceutical formulations using doped GQDs. The spectral characteristics of the GQDs-glimepiride system will be investigated, and the sensing mechanism will be studied using Stern-Volmer analysis and thermodynamic studies. Factors affecting the fluorescence quenching process will be systematically optimized using a Box-Behnken experimental design. The proposed method will be validated according to ICH guidelines and applied for the analysis of glimepiride in spiked human plasma and pharmaceutical formulations. Moreover, Greenness of the proposed method will also be assessed using the Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) tool [25] to underscore its eco-friendly and sustainable nature. Finally, the analytical practicability of the developed method i.e. method blueness will be highlighted using the Blue applicability grade index (BAGI) tool [26] to emphasize its economical and operational advantages in comparison with existing analytical techniques.

Standard glimepiride with certified purity of 99.45 % was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid, thiourea, and all other reagents were of analytical grade and were purchased from Alfa Chemical (China). Pharmaceutical formulations containing glimepiride (Amaryl®) were purchased from a local pharmacy, Cairo, Egypt and claimed to contain 2 mg glimepiride per tablet. HPLC grade acetonitrile was obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) and distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

S,N-doped graphene quantum dots were synthesized following a previously reported method with minor modifications. Briefly, citric acid (0.5 g) and thiourea (0.7 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 180 °C for 5 h in an oven. The resulting S,N-GQDs were recovered by adding ethanol to the solution, followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min. 200 mg of the obtained S,N-GQDs was dispersed in 100 mL distilled water to prepare a stock solution of (2 mg/mL) that can be used for further experiments. Britton-Robinson buffer was used to prepare solutions of different pH values using acetic acid, boric acid, and phosphoric acid. Glimepiride was dissolved in acetonitrile to prepare a standard stock solution labeled to contain 100 μg/mL of the drug.

All fluorescence measurements were performed using a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorometer equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp (Tokyo, Japan). Samples were analyzed in a 1 cm quartz cell at an excitation wavelength of 361 nm and emission wavelength range of 220–600 nm. The slit widths for both excitation and emission were set at 10 nm. pH measurements were carried out using a Jen Way 3510 pH meter. Spectrofluorimetric acquisition and data analysis were performed using Spectra Manager software.

To construct the fluorescent probe, 1.75 mL of the 2 mg/mL S,N-GQDs stock solution was added to a 10 mL volumetric flask, followed by 3 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer with a pH of 8.5. Different volumes of the glimepiride standard solution were then added to obtain the desired concentrations. The final volume was made up to 10 mL with distilled water: acetonitrile (8:2, v/v). The resulting solution was allowed to stand for 3 min before recording the fluorescence spectra using an excitation wavelength of 361 nm and emission wavelength range of 400–600 nm (F). A blank solution was prepared in the same manner but without the addition of glimepiride, and its fluorescence intensity was recorded (F0). The quenching efficiency was calculated as (F0/F), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of glimepiride, respectively.

The developed spectrofluorimetric method was validated according to ICH guidelines Q2(R1) in terms of linearity, limit of detection, limit of quantitation, accuracy, precision, robustness, and selectivity. Linearity was evaluated by constructing a calibration curve using 7 standard solutions in the concentration range of 0.5–4.0 µg/mL of glimepiride. Limits of detection and quantitation were calculated based on the standard deviation of the residual and the slope of the calibration curve according to the following equations:
LOD = 3.3 σ / s
LOQ = 10 σ / s
where σ is the standard deviation of the residual and s is the slope of the calibration curve.

For plasma analysis, a matrix-matched calibration curve was constructed by spiking blank human plasma with glimepiride standard solution to obtain concentrations in the range of 0.5–4.0 μg/mL. The spiked calibration standards were processed by deproteination using 3 mL of acetonitrile followed by vertexing and centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The clear supernatant was transferred to a 10 mL volumetric flask and the optimized S,N-GQDs based fluorescence quenching procedure was applied. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the quenching response (F0/F) versus glimepiride concentration in plasma.

The accuracy of the method was assessed at three concentration levels (1.4, 1.75 and 2.1 μg/mL) representing 80 %, 100 %, and 120 % of the target concentration by analyzing three replicate samples at each level and the recovery percentage (%R) was calculated. The intra-day and inter-day precisions were evaluated at three concentration levels on the same day and three consecutive days, respectively and the %RSD was calculated.

The robustness of the method was evaluated by intentionally making small changes in the pH of the medium (± 0.2), the volume of the S,N-GQDs (± 0.1 mL) and incubation time (± 0.5 min) and the effect on the quenching efficiency was investigated. The selectivity of the method was assessed by analyzing the effect of common excipients that may be present in formulations in addition to common interfering ions and plasma samples. Standard addition technique was also employed to confirm the selectivity of the method by spiking the formulation with known amounts of glimepiride and calculating the recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to pharmaceutical analysis and spiked plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: The validated method was applied to determine the glimepiride content in commercial tablets and spiked human plasma samples. For pharmaceutical formulations, ten tablets were weighed, finely powdered and an amount equivalent to 5 mg of was accurately weighed and transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask. The drug was extracted using acetonitrile, the solution was sonicated for 15 min, and the volume was made up to the mark with the same solvent to obtain a solution labeled to contain 50 μg/mL of glimepiride. Different aliquots of this solution were transferred to 10 mL volumes flasks, where the optimized S,N-GQDs based fluorescence quenching procedure was applied. The content of glimepiride in the tablets was calculated using the regression equation.

For recovery studies in plasma samples, 3 batches of blank human plasma were spiked with known concentrations of glimepiride, and the samples were processed using the same deproteination procedure described above. The concentration of glimepiride in the spiked plasma samples was determined using the plasma-specific calibration curve.

The synthesized S,N-GQDs exhibited strong blue emission with a maximum peak at 431 nm when excited at 362 nm (Fig. 1
A). This characteristic blue emission can be attributed to the interplay between sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the graphene core, further modulated by the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms. The presence of these dopants likely introduces new energy levels and alters the band gap, resulting in the observed optical properties. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis revealed an average particle size of 4.75 nm for the S,N-GQDs ( Fig. S1 ). This nanoscale dimension is consistent with quantum confinement effects, which contribute to the unique optical properties of these quantum dots. Upon the gradual addition of glimepiride, a progressive decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed, indicative of a concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching mechanism (Fig. 1 B).

To further understand the nature of this quenching process, Stern-Volmer analysis was performed where the fluorescence quenching data was fitted to the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0 / F = 1 +
K
SV
Q

where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher (glimepiride), respectively, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. This procedure has been performed at different temperatures (298 K, 303 K, and 313 K) to determine whether the quenching process is static or dynamic in nature in addition to evaluating the thermodynamic parameters ( Table S2 ). The results revealed the KSV values decreased from 4.63 × 105 M−1 at 298 K to 2.86 × 105 M−1 at 313 K, suggestive of a static quenching mechanism. A decrease in the slope of the Stern-Volmer plot with increasing temperature further confirmed the static nature of the quenching (Fig. 2
A). Hence, the binding constant was calculated using the equation:
F 0 /
F 0 - F
= 1 / K a
1 /
Q
+ 1

where Ka is the binding constant and [Q] is the quencher concentration. A decrease in the Ka values from 3.04 × 105 M−1 at 298 K to 2.26 × 105 M−1 at 313 K indicates the binding process is exothermic (ΔH < 0) and might suggest the involvement of hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions in the binding process.

To further corroborate the static quenching mechanism and gain deeper insights into the thermodynamics of the S,N-GQDs-glimepiride interaction, a Van't Hoff analysis was performed. The Van't Hoff plot, which relates the natural logarithm of the binding constant (ln Ka) to the inverse of temperature (1/T), exhibited a linear relationship (Fig. 2 B). The linearity of the Van't Hoff plot not only validates the static quenching mechanism but also enables the accurate determination of key thermodynamic parameters. These parameters include Gibbs free energy, enthalpy, and entropy changes. The negative ΔG° of −31.29 kJ/mol, the negative ΔH° of −15.28 kJ/mol, and the positive ΔS° of 53.72 J/mol K−1 collectively suggest that the binding process is spontaneous, exothermic, and entropy-driven ( Table S2 ). The negative ΔH° confirms the earlier observations from the temperature-dependent Ka values. Interestingly, the positive ΔS° suggests that the binding process is entropy-driven, which may be attributed to the release of ordered water molecules from the solvation shells of both the S,N-GQDs and glimepiride upon complex formation. The combination of negative ΔH° and positive ΔS° implies that both enthalpic and entropic factors contribute favorably to the binding process.

The key fluorescence quenching parameters were systematically optimized using a Box-Behnken experimental design. A quadratic model was derived to correlate the four independent variables, namely, pH of the medium, volume of the buffer added, S,N-GQDs concentration, and incubation time, with the response variable, fluorescence quenching (F0/F). A backward elimination analysis helped identify the most significant factors with factors above 0.05p-value threshold removed in a stepwise manner. A significant mode was attained as indicated by ANOVA analysis with an F-value of 101.77 and p-value less than 0.0001 (Table 1 ). All the four main effects were found significant as well as the interaction between S,N-GQDs concentration and pH. Quadratic terms of pH and S,N-GQDs concentration were also significant according to the analysis. Hence, the final model equation was:
R = 2.00224 + 0.210031 A + 0.169351 B + 0.412407 C + 0.0548696 D + 0.153854 A C - 0.229525
A 2
- 0.119394 ∗
C 2

where R is the fluorescence quenching response, A is the pH, B is the volume of the buffer, C is the S,N-GQDs concentration, and D is the incubation time. The most significant factor was the S,N-GQDs concentration, followed by pH, then the interaction between them, and finally the quadratic terms. The positive signs for the main effects suggest that increasing their levels leads to higher fluorescence quenching, while the negative signs for the quadratic terms indicate that there is an optimal level beyond which further increases do not improve the response. A positive interaction between S,N-GQDs concentration and pH signifies that the effect of one factor is dependent on the level of the other probably due to the charge interactions between the anionic GQDs and the cationic drug model.

Since the pH and S,N-GQDs concentration were the most influential factors, their combined effect was visualized using 2D interaction plot and 3D surface plot (Fig. 3 ). The 2D plot reveals a strong effect of S,N-GQDs concentration on fluorescence quenching, with higher concentrations consistently resulting in greater quenching (higher F0/F values) across all pH levels (Fig. 3 A). The curvature of the lines demonstrates the quadratic effect of pH, with quenching increasing as pH rises from 5 to about 7–8, then slightly decreasing or plateauing. The non-parallel nature of the curves confirms the significant interaction between pH and S,N-GQDs concentration, particularly evident at higher S,N-GQDs concentrations (Fig. 3 A). The 3D surface plot reinforces these observations, showing maximum quenching at the highest S,N-GQDs and alkaline pH values (Fig. 3 B). The curved surface illustrates the quadratic effects of both factors, while the steeper gradient along the S,N-GQDs concentration axis visually confirms its stronger effect on quenching compared to pH (Fig. 3 B).

The analytical method for the determination of glimepiride using the S,N-GQDs based fluorescence sensor was validated according to the ICH guidelines. The method showed a good linearity over the concentration range of 0.5 to 4.0 μg/mL, with a coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.9996 (Table 2 ). The limit of detection and limit of quantification were found to be 0.0780 μg/mL and 0.2364 μg/mL, respectively, indicating a high sensitivity of the developed method. It is worth noting that these LOD and LOQ values are lower than those reported in the literature for other analytical techniques used for glimepiride determination particularly spectrophotometric and HPLC methods coupled with UV detection.

The accuracy of the method was evaluated at three concentration levels 80 %, 100 %, and 120 % of the target concentration by measuring the recovery of triplicate samples. The results showed that the average was 98.48 %, with relative standard deviations less than 1 %, fulfilling the ICH acceptance criteria (Table 2). Precision in terms of repeatability and intermediate precision met the acceptance limits, with %RSD values of less than 1.5 % for intraday and interday precisions. The robustness of the method was assessed by deliberately varying the experimental parameters such as pH, S,N-GQDs concentration and incubation time. The fluorescence response was found to be robust to these minor variations, with %RSD values less than 2 %, confirming the reliability of the proposed method (Table 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to pharmaceutical formulations and plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: The validated method was successfully applied for the determination of glimepiride in commercially available tablets and in human plasma samples. The drug concentration in the tablet formulation was found to be 99.78 % of the labeled claim, indicating the accuracy of the proposed method for the quantification of glimepiride in pharmaceutical preparations (Table 3 ). Low variance was also observed between different tablet samples, confirming the method's precision. Additionally, statistical comparison of the results obtained by the S,N-GQDs based method and a reference HPLC method [20] showed no significant difference, further validating the reliability of the proposed analytical approach (Table 3).

The major advantage of the proposed analytical method is its “green” and “blue” chemistry attributes. The use of biocompatible and non–toxic S,N–GQDs as the sensing material, and the avoidance of organic solvents and hazardous reagents, make this approach more environmentally friendly compared to traditional analytical techniques, such as HPLC or spectrophotometry, which often involve the use of toxic solvents and chemicals. Additionally, the employed detection technique, i.e. spectrofluorimetry requires minimal energy consumption, further enhancing the sustainability of the developed method. To quantitatively evaluate the greenness, the method was assessed using the AGREE tool which is based on evaluating the 12 principles of green chemistry. The AGREE score obtained for the proposed S,N–GQDs–based method was 0.73, indicating a high degree of greenness (Fig. 5
A). On the other hand, the AGREE score for a reported HPLC based method for glimepiride analysis [20] is 0.62 (Fig. 5 B) and for the LC-MS/MS [21] is 0.6 (Fig. 5 C), confirming the improved environmental credentials of the fluorescence sensor–based technique.

The method practicability was evaluated using the BAGI tool which measures the blueness attributes in terms of cost, time, and energy. Interestingly, the BAGI score for the S,N–GQDs method as well as the reported HPLC and LC-MS/MS was all 75 (Fig. 5 D-F), indicating a similar practical applicability. Although the instrumentation cost for the fluorescence–based technique is lower compared to the chromatographic techniques, the chromatographic methods transcended in their ability to be automated and faster sample throughput especially in the LC-MS/MS method, balancing the overall practical aspects. Overall the proposed S,N-GQDs based sensor provides a green, cost-effective, and sensitive alternative to the traditional analytical techniques for the determination of glimepiride in pharmaceutical and biological samples.

In summary, a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor based on S,N-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) have been developed and validated for the determination of glimepiride in pharmaceutical formulations and human plasma samples. A static quenching mechanism was observed, and the impact of various experimental parameters on the sensing performance was optimized using a Box-Behnken design. The developed method exhibited excellent linearity, accuracy, and precision, meeting the ICH validation criteria. The method was successfully applied for the quantification of glimepiride in commercial tablets and spiked human plasma samples, with recovery values within the acceptable range. Importantly, the proposed approach demonstrates improved environmental credentials compared to the traditional analytical techniques, making it a greener and more sustainable alternative for the analysis of pharmaceuticals.

Future studies could explore the potential of the S,N-GQDs sensor for real-time monitoring of glimepiride in biological fluids, which would be highly beneficial for therapeutic drug monitoring and pharmacokinetic studies. Additionally, the selectivity of the sensor could be further enhanced by incorporating molecular imprinting or other advanced recognition elements to expand its applicability to more complex matrices. Environmental monitoring of glimepiride and other antidiabetic drugs in water bodies is another promising area of research that could benefit from the developed fluorescence-based analytical method.
This research was funded by Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (RGP2/399/45).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Research Project under grant numberRGP2/399/45.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125820.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
